title: Microscopic Colitis
oneLiner: Chronic watery, non-bloody diarrhoea; normal mucosa on endoscopy; biopsy confirms collagenous or lymphocytic colitis; most common cause of chronic diarrhoea in elderly women.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Severe dehydration / hypovolaemic shock** – single finding: **K+ < 3.0 mmol/L or acute kidney injury AKI stage 3 (creatinine >3× baseline)**. Before treating the diarrhoea, correct volume and electrolytes.
- **Infection (especially Clostridioides difficile)** – single finding: **stool toxin A/B positive**. Do not start steroids until C. diff is excluded; steroids in active C. diff can cause toxic megacolon.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Chronic watery, non-bloody diarrhoea (≥3 loose stools/day, >4 weeks)
  - Nocturnal stools (feature that distinguishes from IBS)
  - Urgency, faecal incontinence
  - Weight loss (mild, 2–5 kg)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Generally unremarkable
  - Mild lower abdominal tenderness possible
  - No perianal disease, no blood on PR (red flag for IBD)
  - Evidence of dehydration (skin turgor, dry mucous membranes, postural hypotension)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely, with 1-line differentiators:**
  - **Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS-D):** chronic, but not nocturnal; no weight loss; normal inflammatory markers and calprotectin.
  - **Bile acid malabsorption:** watery diarrhoea after cholecystectomy or ileal resection; trial of cholestyramine helps.
  - **Infective colitis (C. diff, Giardia, Campylobacter):** acute onset, ± fever, bloody stools; stool culture/MC&S positive.
  - **Inflammatory bowel disease (UC / Crohn's):** blood ± mucus, elevated CRP/calprotectin, abnormal mucosa on endoscopy.
  - **Coeliac disease:** bloating, steatorrhoea, iron deficiency; anti-tTG positive.
- **Splitter:**
  - **No blood + nocturnal diarrhoea + normal CRP and calprotectin → favour microscopic colitis, not IBD.**
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Stool chart (frequency, consistency, blood)
  - Urine dip for ketones / SG for hydration status
  - Capillary blood glucose if on steroids or diabetic
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (check for anaemia, eosinophils)
  - U&Es (K+, Mg²⁺, Ca²⁺ – often low)
  - CRP, ESR (usually normal)
  - Faecal calprotectin (normal in microscopic colitis; elevated in IBD)
  - Coeliac serology (anti-tTG IgA; if deficient, total IgA)
  - TFTs (autoimmune link)
- **Imaging:**
  - **None routine** unless suspect obstruction or severe IBD; then AXR for toxic megacolon.
- **Special tests:**
  - **Flexible sigmoidoscopy with biopsies** – gold standard; normal mucosa endoscopically; biopsies show collagen band (>10 µm) or intraepithelial lymphocytes (>20 per 100 epithelial cells).
  - Right colon biopsies increase yield (24% patchy disease).
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **Collagenous colitis:** subepithelial collagen band >10 µm + chronic inflammation.
  - **Lymphocytic colitis:** intraepithelial lymphocytes >20/100 enterocytes + surface epithelial damage.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Stool culture + C. diff toxin** – before starting any steroid or immunosuppression. Also **coeliac serology** (treatable mimic).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - IV fluids (crystalloid, e.g. Hartmann's) if hypovolaemic
  - Correct K⁺ / Mg²⁺
  - Stop any provoking drugs: NSAIDs, PPIs, SSRIs, statins, beta-blockers (common triggers)
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - **First-line drug:** budesonide 9 mg PO once daily (MMX or "Budenofalk"–type) for 8–12 weeks
  - Consider antidiarrhoeals: loperamide 2 mg after each loose stool (max 16 mg/day) *only after infection excluded*
  - If budesonide contraindicated or fails: cholestyramine 4 g PO once to twice daily (start low, titrate)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Budesonide tapering regimen: 9 mg x 8 weeks → 6 mg x 4 weeks → 3 mg x 2 weeks, then stop
  - Relapse management: repeat course or low-dose maintenance (3–6 mg daily) under GI supervision
  - Review and stop causative drugs
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% NaCl + glucose 5% at 125 mL/h, add K⁺ replacement per deficit
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH if ≥2 risk factors
  - Diet: normal, but avoid high-fat meals
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (or 1-hourly if NEWS ≥5)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line – budesonide (Entocort / Budenofalk):**
  - 9 mg PO once daily for 8 weeks, then taper.
  - *Dose adjustment:* none for renal or hepatic impairment (first-pass metabolism minimises systemic effect).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Loperamide 2 mg PO after each loose stool (max 16 mg/day)
  - Cholestyramine 4 g PO 1–2 times daily (start at 4 g daily, titrate)
  - Bismuth subsalicylate 2 tablets PO QDS (less commonly used in UK)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Budesonide: caution with CYP3A4 inhibitors (azole antifungals, macrolides); avoid live vaccines.
  - Loperamide: contraindicated if active infection or bloody stools; can cause toxic megacolon in IBD.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Cholestyramine: reduces absorption of fat-soluble vitamins and other drugs; give 2 h apart from other meds.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Starting any steroid (budesonide or prednisolone) without stool cultures for C. diff** – can precipitate severe colitis / toxic megacolon.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, dehydration, AKI
  - Loperamide toxicity: ileus, central nervous system depression (especially in elderly)
  - C. diff superinfection if steroids used mistakenly
- **Chronic:**
  - Weight loss, malnutrition, vitamin deficiencies (B12, D, iron)
  - Relapsing-remitting course (up to 80% relapse within 1–2 years)
  - Rarely: lymphocytic colitis associated with microscopic forms of IBD / autoimmune hepatitis
sections.Prognosis: - Chronic, non-fatal condition. 60–80% respond to budesonide, but 50–80% relapse after discontinuation. Long-term remission possible with drug withdrawal and dietary/trigger avoidance. No increased cancer risk.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always take right colon biopsies** – 24% of cases show isolated right-sided disease; a normal sigmoid doesn't exclude it.
- **Stop NSAIDs and PPIs** immediately – they are the commonest drug triggers, and withdrawal alone can resolve symptoms in 30%.
- **Consider bile acid malabsorption** if no response to budesonide – test with SeHCAT scan (retention <10% confirms).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not label as IBS without biopsy** – especially in older patients with nocturnal diarrhoea and normal calprotectin.
- **Do not give loperamide empirically** before stool cultures – may mask C. diff and worsen infection.
- **Do not prescribe systemic steroids (prednisolone) first-line** – budesonide has much fewer side effects and is equivalent efficacy.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening frequency (>10 stools/day)
  - Blood in stool
  - Inability to keep down oral fluids, or signs of dehydration (dizziness, reduced urine output)
  - Abdominal pain or distension (rule out megacolon or C. diff)
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP in 2 weeks for symptom review and to stop provoking drugs
  - Gastroenterology outpatient follow-up after 8 weeks to assess biopsy results and plan budesonide taper
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / GI reg if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 despite fluid resuscitation
  - AKI stage 2 or 3 (creatinine >2× baseline)
  - K+ < 2.5 mmol/L or Mg²⁺ < 0.5 mmol/L
  - Suspected toxic megacolon (abdominal distension, tympany, fever, bloody diarrhoea)
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - Sepsis (qSOFA ≥2) not responding to IV fluids
  - Uncorrected hypovolaemic shock (lactate >4 mmol/L, MAP <65 mmHg despite 30 mL/kg crystalloid)
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - NEWS2: ≥5 → escalate for senior review
  - Lactate: >2 mmol/L → consider IV fluids and discuss with medical registrar
  - GCS: <15 → assess for electrolyte encephalopathy or sepsis

rawMarkdown:
## Microscopic Colitis
**One-liner:** Chronic watery, non-bloody diarrhoea; normal mucosa on endoscopy; biopsy confirms collagenous or lymphocytic colitis; most common cause of chronic diarrhoea in elderly women.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Severe dehydration / hypovolaemic shock** – single finding: **K+ < 3.0 mmol/L or acute kidney injury AKI stage 3 (creatinine >3× baseline)**. Before treating the diarrhoea, correct volume and electrolytes.
- **Infection (especially Clostridioides difficile)** – single finding: **stool toxin A/B positive**. Do not start steroids until C. diff is excluded; steroids in active C. diff can cause toxic megacolon.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Chronic watery, non-bloody diarrhoea (≥3 loose stools/day, >4 weeks)
  - Nocturnal stools (feature that distinguishes from IBS)
  - Urgency, faecal incontinence
  - Weight loss (mild, 2–5 kg)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Generally unremarkable
  - Mild lower abdominal tenderness possible
  - No perianal disease, no blood on PR (red flag for IBD)
  - Evidence of dehydration (skin turgor, dry mucous membranes, postural hypotension)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely, with 1-line differentiators:**
  - **Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS-D):** chronic, but not nocturnal; no weight loss; normal inflammatory markers and calprotectin.
  - **Bile acid malabsorption:** watery diarrhoea after cholecystectomy or ileal resection; trial of cholestyramine helps.
  - **Infective colitis (C. diff, Giardia, Campylobacter):** acute onset, ± fever, bloody stools; stool culture/MC&S positive.
  - **Inflammatory bowel disease (UC / Crohn's):** blood ± mucus, elevated CRP/calprotectin, abnormal mucosa on endoscopy.
  - **Coeliac disease:** bloating, steatorrhoea, iron deficiency; anti-tTG positive.
- **Splitter:**
  - **No blood + nocturnal diarrhoea + normal CRP and calprotectin → favour microscopic colitis, not IBD.**

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Stool chart (frequency, consistency, blood)
  - Urine dip for ketones / SG for hydration status
  - Capillary blood glucose if on steroids or diabetic
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (check for anaemia, eosinophils)
  - U&Es (K+, Mg²⁺, Ca²⁺ – often low)
  - CRP, ESR (usually normal)
  - Faecal calprotectin (normal in microscopic colitis; elevated in IBD)
  - Coeliac serology (anti-tTG IgA; if deficient, total IgA)
  - TFTs (autoimmune link)
- **Imaging:**
  - **None routine** unless suspect obstruction or severe IBD; then AXR for toxic megacolon.
- **Special tests:**
  - **Flexible sigmoidoscopy with biopsies** – gold standard; normal mucosa endoscopically; biopsies show collagen band (>10 µm) or intraepithelial lymphocytes (>20 per 100 epithelial cells).
  - Right colon biopsies increase yield (24% patchy disease).
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - **Collagenous colitis:** subepithelial collagen band >10 µm + chronic inflammation.
  - **Lymphocytic colitis:** intraepithelial lymphocytes >20/100 enterocytes + surface epithelial damage.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Stool culture + C. diff toxin** – before starting any steroid or immunosuppression. Also **coeliac serology** (treatable mimic).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - IV fluids (crystalloid, e.g. Hartmann's) if hypovolaemic
  - Correct K⁺ / Mg²⁺
  - Stop any provoking drugs: NSAIDs, PPIs, SSRIs, statins, beta-blockers (common triggers)
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - **First-line drug:** budesonide 9 mg PO once daily (MMX or "Budenofalk"–type) for 8–12 weeks
  - Consider antidiarrhoeals: loperamide 2 mg after each loose stool (max 16 mg/day) *only after infection excluded*
  - If budesonide contraindicated or fails: cholestyramine 4 g PO once to twice daily (start low, titrate)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Budesonide tapering regimen: 9 mg x 8 weeks → 6 mg x 4 weeks → 3 mg x 2 weeks, then stop
  - Relapse management: repeat course or low-dose maintenance (3–6 mg daily) under GI supervision
  - Review and stop causative drugs
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% NaCl + glucose 5% at 125 mL/h, add K⁺ replacement per deficit
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH if ≥2 risk factors
  - Diet: normal, but avoid high-fat meals
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly (or 1-hourly if NEWS ≥5)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line – budesonide (Entocort / Budenofalk):**
  - 9 mg PO once daily for 8 weeks, then taper.
  - *Dose adjustment:* none for renal or hepatic impairment (first-pass metabolism minimises systemic effect).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Loperamide 2 mg PO after each loose stool (max 16 mg/day)
  - Cholestyramine 4 g PO 1–2 times daily (start at 4 g daily, titrate)
  - Bismuth subsalicylate 2 tablets PO QDS (less commonly used in UK)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Budesonide: caution with CYP3A4 inhibitors (azole antifungals, macrolides); avoid live vaccines.
  - Loperamide: contraindicated if active infection or bloody stools; can cause toxic megacolon in IBD.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Cholestyramine: reduces absorption of fat-soluble vitamins and other drugs; give 2 h apart from other meds.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Starting any steroid (budesonide or prednisolone) without stool cultures for C. diff** – can precipitate severe colitis / toxic megacolon.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, dehydration, AKI
  - Loperamide toxicity: ileus, central nervous system depression (especially in elderly)
  - C. diff superinfection if steroids used mistakenly
- **Chronic:**
  - Weight loss, malnutrition, vitamin deficiencies (B12, D, iron)
  - Relapsing-remitting course (up to 80% relapse within 1–2 years)
  - Rarely: lymphocytic colitis associated with microscopic forms of IBD / autoimmune hepatitis

### Prognosis
- Chronic, non-fatal condition. 60–80% respond to budesonide, but 50–80% relapse after discontinuation. Long-term remission possible with drug withdrawal and dietary/trigger avoidance. No increased cancer risk.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always take right colon biopsies** – 24% of cases show isolated right-sided disease; a normal sigmoid doesn't exclude it.
- **Stop NSAIDs and PPIs** immediately – they are the commonest drug triggers, and withdrawal alone can resolve symptoms in 30%.
- **Consider bile acid malabsorption** if no response to budesonide – test with SeHCAT scan (retention <10% confirms).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not label as IBS without biopsy** – especially in older patients with nocturnal diarrhoea and normal calprotectin.
- **Do not give loperamide empirically** before stool cultures – may mask C. diff and worsen infection.
- **Do not prescribe systemic steroids (prednisolone) first-line** – budesonide has much fewer side effects and is equivalent efficacy.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening frequency (>10 stools/day)
  - Blood in stool
  - Inability to keep down oral fluids, or signs of dehydration (dizziness, reduced urine output)
  - Abdominal pain or distension (rule out megacolon or C. diff)
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP in 2 weeks for symptom review and to stop provoking drugs
  - Gastroenterology outpatient follow-up after 8 weeks to assess biopsy results and plan budesonide taper

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / GI reg if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 despite fluid resuscitation
  - AKI stage 2 or 3 (creatinine >2× baseline)
  - K+ < 2.5 mmol/L or Mg²⁺ < 0.5 mmol/L
  - Suspected toxic megacolon (abdominal distension, tympany, fever, bloody diarrhoea)
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - Sepsis (qSOFA ≥2) not responding to IV fluids
  - Uncorrected hypovolaemic shock (lactate >4 mmol/L, MAP <65 mmHg despite 30 mL/kg crystalloid)
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - NEWS2: ≥5 → escalate for senior review
  - Lactate: >2 mmol/L → consider IV fluids and discuss with medical registrar
  - GCS: <15 → assess for electrolyte encephalopathy or sepsis
